title: Designing Machine Learning Systems
subtitle: An Iterative Process for Production-Ready Applications
author: Chip Huyen

sections.title: Overview of Machine Learning Systems
sections.desc: When ML is the right tool, where it shines, and how production ML differs from research and traditional software.
sections.lede: Before writing a single model, you need to know when machine learning is worth it — and how an ML system in production is a very different beast from a Kaggle notebook.

sections.title: Introduction to Machine Learning Systems Design
sections.desc: Aligning ML with business goals, the four system requirements, the iterative design loop, framing problems, and the data-vs-algorithms debate.
sections.lede: A great model that doesn't move a business metric is a failed project. This chapter turns fuzzy goals into a well-framed ML problem with clear requirements.

sections.title: Data Engineering Fundamentals
sections.desc: Data sources, formats, relational and NoSQL models, OLTP vs OLAP, ETL, dataflow modes, and batch vs stream processing.
sections.lede: ML runs on data, and data runs on infrastructure. Here are the storage, format, and dataflow fundamentals every ML engineer should own.

sections.title: Training Data
sections.desc: Sampling strategies, hand and natural labels, weak/semi/active learning, class imbalance, and data augmentation.
sections.lede: Training data is created, not found. This chapter covers how to sample it, label it (even when labels are scarce), and balance and augment it.

sections.title: Feature Engineering
sections.desc: Learned vs engineered features, common operations, the danger of data leakage, and what makes a feature good.
sections.lede: Having the right features often beats having the fanciest model. This chapter covers the craft — and the subtle leakage traps that silently inflate your scores.

sections.title: Model Development and Offline Evaluation
sections.desc: Selecting models, ensembles, experiment tracking, distributed training, AutoML, baselines, and evaluation methods.
sections.lede: How to choose, train, track, and rigorously evaluate models — including the offline checks that tell you whether a model is actually ready to face the world.

sections.title: Model Deployment and Prediction Service
sections.desc: Deployment myths, batch vs online prediction, model compression, and serving ML on the cloud and the edge.
sections.lede: Deploying a model is where research becomes a product. This chapter covers serving patterns, making models small and fast, and the cloud-versus-edge decision.

sections.title: Data Distribution Shifts and Monitoring
sections.desc: Why ML systems fail, the types of distribution shift, how to detect them, and monitoring vs observability.
sections.lede: Models decay because the world changes. This chapter explains the failure modes unique to ML, how distributions drift, and how to watch for it in production.

sections.title: Continual Learning and Test in Production
sections.desc: Stateless vs stateful retraining, the four stages of continual learning, and safe ways to test models on live traffic.
sections.lede: The antidote to staleness is updating models continually — and the only honest test is production itself. Here's how to do both safely.

sections.title: Infrastructure and Tooling for MLOps
sections.desc: Storage and compute, dev environments, containers, orchestration, the ML platform, and build-vs-buy.
sections.lede: The unglamorous foundation that lets teams ship ML reliably: compute, environments, orchestration, model and feature stores, and what to build versus buy.

sections.title: The Human Side of Machine Learning
sections.desc: Designing for user experience, structuring ML teams, and a practical framework for responsible AI.
sections.lede: ML systems are built by people, for people. This chapter covers user experience under uncertainty, how to organize teams, and building AI responsibly.
